key_insight: A system-specific MACE model trained on SQS DFT data describes Fe-Ni alloy structure, elasticity, and thermal trends more accurately than foundation models, but still misses magnetic-collapse-driven phase stability under pressure.
engineering_relevance: Targeted retraining can make machine-learned interatomic potentials reliable for alloy property prediction, helping accelerate materials design for steels and magnetic alloys.
method: Spin-polarized PBE DFT benchmark on disordered SQS supercells
Train a MACE-sqs potential on bcc/fcc compositions plus volumetric and shear deformations
Compare against several MACE foundation models with and without Hubbard U corrections
evidence_signal: The MACE-sqs model achieved 2.0 meV/atom validation energy error and 24.3 meV/Å force error, and showed the best agreement with DFT and experiment for equations of state, equilibrium volumes, elastic constants, and thermal expansion.
watch_item: All models predicted the wrong trend for the bcc-to-hcp transition pressure with Ni content, showing that magnetic collapse and magnetoelastic effects are still not fully captured.
paper_title: Can MACE Potentials Accurately Describe Magnetism and Phase Stability in Fe-Ni Alloys? A Systematic Benchmark
